name: hypericum perforatum
mechanism: Likely pharmacologic activity is multifactorial (including effects on monoamine signaling and drug-metabolizing enzymes). Interaction liability is well established and clinically important.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Use with SSRIs, pregnancy, strong sunlight exposure
safetyNotes: St. John's wort can cause clinically significant herb-drug interactions and should not be self-combined with prescription antidepressants. Use in pregnancy is generally avoided because of potential fetal risk signals.

sources.title: NCCIH: St. John's Wort and Depression — In Depth

description: Hypericum perforatum preparations vary substantially by extract profile. Clinical outcomes are indication- and product-dependent.
dosage: Clinical studies are mostly short term (often up to about 12 weeks) with standardized extracts; dosing varies by product.

interactions: Can reduce effectiveness of many medicines (including some antidepressants, oral contraceptives, warfarin, immunosuppressants, seizure medicines, HIV therapies, and some cancer therapies).
Combining with serotonergic medicines can increase risk of serotonin-related adverse effects.
preparation: Commercial use is primarily oral extracts from flowering aerial parts.
region: Native to Europe and parts of Asia; now naturalized in many temperate regions.
therapeuticUses: Mild to moderate depression, anxiety, sleep issues
summary: St. John's wort is most studied for depression symptoms; evidence suggests possible benefit for mild to moderate depression, with uncertainty for severe depression or long-term use.
